record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk and Recorder (deeds, mortgages, liens, plats -- one office per county)
record_office_terminology.probate: District Court (probate, wills, estates, guardianships -- Colorado uses district courts for probate, not separate probate courts)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk and Recorder (marriage licenses from territorial period; certificates)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1907 (statewide vital records registration began; earlier births/deaths found in county, church, and cemetery records; some counties have earlier local registration)

burned_county_authority: Colorado State Archives, Denver -- Colorado has no significant burned county courthouse losses. Record survival is generally good across all 64 counties, though some early territorial records are incomplete due to the informal nature of early mining camp record-keeping rather than fire or disaster. Colorado State Archives holds microfilm copies of many county records.
administrative_notes: Colorado became a territory in 1861 (carved from Kansas, Nebraska, New Mexico, and Utah territories) and achieved statehood on August 1, 1876 (the Centennial State). The 1858-1859 Pike's Peak gold rush drove initial settlement, concentrated along the Front Range and in mountain mining camps. The original 17 territorial counties have been subdivided into the current 64, with Broomfield (2001) being the most recent addition. Colorado is a public land state -- BLM General Land Office records (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) are essential for land research. The state has diverse heritage: Native American (Ute, Arapaho, Cheyenne), Spanish colonial (San Luis Valley), mining-era immigrants (Cornish, Irish, Italian, Scandinavian, Chinese, Japanese), agricultural immigrants (German-Russian Volga Germans, Hispanic workers), and African American communities (Denver's Five Points neighborhood). The County Clerk and Recorder handles land and marriage records; the District Court handles probate. Colorado State Archives and History Colorado (formerly Colorado Historical Society) in Denver are the primary state-level repositories. The Denver Public Library's Western History and Genealogy Department is one of the premier genealogical research centers in the Rocky Mountain West.

core_repositories.description: Colorado's official state archives holding county records on microfilm (deeds, marriages, probate, court records), territorial records from 1861, state government records, military records (including Colorado National Guard and veteran records), vital records, prison records (Colorado State Penitentiary from 1871), and naturalization records. Essential for all Colorado genealogical research.

Major manuscript repository holding personal papers, business records, mining company records, photograph collections, maps, and oral histories. The Stephen H. Hart Research Center has genealogy-relevant collections including family papers, county histories, cemetery records, and ethnic community documentation. Great Western Sugar Company records and CF&I steelworks materials.

core_repositories.source_name: Denver Public Library -- Western History and Genealogy
core_repositories.description: One of the premier genealogical research centers in the Rocky Mountain West. Holds Denver city directories (from 1859), Colorado newspaper microfilm and indexes, photograph collections (over 600,000 images), manuscript collections, maps, and published genealogies. Western History collection covers the entire Rocky Mountain region. Blair-Caldwell African American Research Library is a branch specializing in African American history.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Colorado vital records (births, deaths, marriages), county clerk records, district court records, cemetery records, and church records. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at Colorado State Archives and county offices. Key collections include Colorado marriages (1858-1939), Colorado deaths (1900-1939), and county land records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Colorado's unique record types (mining claims, BLM patents, territorial records) and provides county-by-county research strategies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Free online access to digitized Colorado newspapers from the 1850s to the present. Includes hundreds of titles from across the state -- mining camp papers, agricultural weeklies, Denver dailies, and ethnic newspapers. Searchable full text. Essential for obituaries, legal notices, marriage and death announcements, and community news.
Digitized historic Colorado newspapers through the Library of Congress. Includes territorial-era and early statehood papers. Complements CHNC with additional titles and different date coverage.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: African American History in Colorado -- Blair-Caldwell Library / History Colorado
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Blair-Caldwell African American Research Library, Denver / History Colorado
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Colorado was a free territory/state but had a significant African American community, particularly in Denver's Five Points neighborhood. Blair-Caldwell Library specializes in African American history of Colorado and the West. History Colorado holds records of Black communities including church records, organizational papers, and photographs. Colorado had African American miners, cowboys, soldiers (Buffalo Soldiers at Fort Lewis and Fort Garland), and business owners.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Colorado Native American Records -- Ute, Arapaho, Cheyenne
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA, Denver / Southern Ute Indian Tribe / Ute Mountain Ute Tribe / History Colorado
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Colorado was homeland to the Ute (western slope), Arapaho, and Cheyenne (eastern plains) peoples. NARA Denver holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records including agency records, census rolls, allotment records, and correspondence. The Sand Creek Massacre (1864) is one of the most significant events in Colorado Native American history. Southern Ute and Ute Mountain Ute reservations in southwestern Colorado maintain tribal records. History Colorado has Native American collections. The Ute Indian Museum in Montrose documents Ute history.

immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from Colorado district courts held at Colorado State Archives and NARA Denver. Colorado attracted diverse immigrants: Cornish and Irish miners, German-Russian Volga German farmers, Italian and Slavic coal miners and steelworkers, Japanese agricultural workers, Chinese miners and railroad workers, Greek workers, Scandinavian miners, and Hispanic settlers from New Mexico. NARA Denver holds federal court naturalization records. County district courts also granted naturalization.

military_records.source_name: Colorado Military Records -- State Archives / NARA
military_records.description: Colorado State Archives holds Colorado National Guard records, adjutant general records, and veteran records. Colorado contributed troops to the Civil War (Colorado Volunteer Infantry and Cavalry, 1861-1865), Spanish-American War, and both World Wars. Fort Logan, Fort Collins, Fort Garland, Fort Lewis, Fort Lyon, Fort Crawford, and Fort Sedgwick records at NARA. WWI and WWII draft registrations. Colorado's military installations (Fort Carson, Lowry AFB, Fitzsimons Army Medical Center, Peterson AFB, USAF Academy) document 20th-century military families.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records -- Colorado
land_and_property.description: Colorado is a public land state -- federal land patents are the foundation of private land ownership. BLM GLO records include homestead entries (Homestead Act 1862), preemption claims, timber culture entries, desert land entries, mineral patents (mining claims), and railroad land grants. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov with full document images. Essential for tracking original settlers and miners in every Colorado county.

land_and_property.source_name: Colorado County Land Records -- County Clerk and Recorder Offices
land_and_property.repository: Individual county Clerk and Recorder offices
land_and_property.description: Each of Colorado's 64 counties has a County Clerk and Recorder office that maintains deed books, mortgages, liens, plats, and mineral rights records. Records date from county formation. Colorado State Archives has microfilm copies of many county deed books.

vital_records.source_name: Colorado Vital Records -- CDPHE / Colorado State Archives
vital_records.repository: Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment / Colorado State Archives
vital_records.description: Statewide vital records registration began in 1907 (births and deaths). Marriage records from County Clerk and Recorder offices from territorial period (1861+). Pre-1907 births and deaths must be researched through church records, cemetery records, newspaper notices, and county records. Colorado State Archives holds historical vital records. FamilySearch has indexed Colorado vital records.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Colorado State Archives / History Colorado / Local congregations and dioceses
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are critical for pre-1907 vital events in Colorado. Catholic parish records (especially in Hispanic communities of the San Luis Valley from the 1850s) are among the oldest in the state. Protestant denominations (Methodist, Presbyterian, Baptist, Episcopal, Lutheran) established congregations in mining camps and towns from the 1860s. Find A Grave and BillionGraves have growing coverage of Colorado cemeteries. Colorado State Archives holds some church record microfilm.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Colorado. Coverage varies by county. Mining town cemeteries, rural family plots, and urban cemeteries included. Fairmount Cemetery and Riverside Cemetery in Denver have notable historical burials. Ghost town and abandoned mining camp cemeteries are partially documented.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing the Colorado Genealogist journal. Maintains surname files, Bible record collections, cemetery indexes, and research guides for all 64 counties. Library holdings at Denver Public Library partnership. Sponsors workshops and educational programs.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 64 Colorado counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, county histories, and research guides.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Colorado has one extinct county: Greenwood County (1870-1874), which was merged into Elbert County. The original 17 territorial counties (1861) have been subdivided into the current 64 counties over time, but Greenwood is the only one that was eliminated. Researchers looking for Greenwood County records should check Colorado State Archives and Elbert County. Additionally, several Colorado counties have been renamed: Carbonate County became Lake County, and the original Summit County was much larger than the present county. The unorganized territories of eastern Colorado were gradually organized into counties from the 1870s through the 1910s.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Colorado has no significant burned county courthouse losses. Record survival across the state is generally good, though some early territorial records from the 1860s are incomplete due to the informal nature of mining camp record-keeping rather than fire or disaster. The Colorado State Archives has proactively microfilmed many county records, providing backup copies. Some mining-era records were lost when mining towns were abandoned, but courthouse records have survived well.
